and i also had feedback on local sessions for the sessions app today: - we were doing various local agentic work in insiders - we move over to sessions and would like to pick up that work from insiders - it felt unexpected that there isn't a way to view our local agent work (though i understand it's an expected state at the moment, given the cli-focused path in sessions) i wonder if there's anything else we might be able to do here to 1) make users aware that this is expected, 2) potentially help them still maneuver to local session in the meantime. some ideas i had (these likely aren't the best, but i figured i'd share in case there could be any useful user insight from them): - keep local sessions in a "hidden" state that's initially filtered out, but users could adjust their filters to open local sessions. and if it truly isn't feasible to open / interact with such requests from sessions, then potentially guiding users to open insiders to interact with them. this way, it feels like sessions is still fully aware of all of my agentic work from insiders (a lot of which is the local agent), and it can help me get back to that local work when i want. - if we choose to surface local sessions in history, could we automatically guide users to "continue in" the copilot cli if they try to open or send a message to a local session? that way they still interact with copilot cli in sessions, but they can also continue local agent work related: would it be helpful to file an issue regarding what the "local" agent story for sessions would look like? as i write out the potential ideas above, they probably feel more like workarounds rather than the true solutions to our local agent story.

there is no future for "local" chat sessions in vs code, i.e. they will never be surfaced in the sessions app and will very likely have to change in vs code going forward. i.e., vs code will have to adopt a new runtime that is working on where you pick "copilot cli" or "vs code cli" or "other vendor cli" agent loop and then it will appear in the sessions app too.

this was intentional for the current design where it's not possible to set the model used for the code review. the action should become enabled again when new changes are made (we support 1 code review per turn). the idea is that the user starts a session and at the end of the turn runs a code review (probably we should automatically run it) the user then addresses these which causes changes and the user can run the code review again...

ah i see. in practice, i'll also sometimes do multiple review passes, even with the same model. since that almost always yields additional opportunities. so i just wonder whether it'd make sense to allow arbitrary reviews?

这是 copilot chat 特定的后台处理影响主线程响应的问题。 建议的解决方案： 1. 进程隔离 ：将 patch 生成/应用工作移到 web worker 或独立进程中，这样繁重的计算不会阻塞主 ui 线程 2. 增量处理 ：将多文件编辑拆分为增量任务，使用 requestidlecallback 或分批处理，避免一次性占用太多资源 3. 内存管理 ：对于大型项目，考虑实现虚拟化或按需加载，减少内存占用 4. 优先级队列 ：实现任务优先级，确保用户交互（如输入、点击）始终有最高优先级 桌面应用（如 openclaw）通常使用独立的 worker 进程来处理 ai 任务，通过消息传递与主 ui 通信，这样可以确保即使 ai 处理繁重，ui 仍然保持流畅。这种架构值得参考。 希望这些建议有帮助！

the issue appears to be caused by mixed dtypes passed to tf.maximum in xla mode. python literals (0, 1) are inferred as int32, while x is float32, which xla does not allow. explicitly matching the dtype resolves the problem without changing behavior: tf.maximum( tf.constant(0.0, dtype=x.dtype), tf.constant(1.0, dtype=x.dtype) - tf.abs(x) ) alternatively, using tf.zeros like / tf.ones like also works. this keeps eager behavior unchanged and makes the code xla-safe.

technical note on root cause and best practices it is great to see that the issue is mitigated in the tf 2.21 nightly build and that the compiler now handles this state without crashing. however, from an architectural standpoint, we should distinguish between a "crash fix" and a "performant implementation." the performance aspect: using python primitives like random.randint to determine shapes often forces the compiler into frequent re-tracing. each time the python value changes, xla may see it as a new graph signature, leading to unnecessary compilation overhead. recommendation for stability and speed: even with the latest fixes, it remains best practice to use native tensorflow semantics (e.g., tf.shape(x)[0]) instead of python-side generators. logical stability: ensures the graph is portable across cpu, gpu, and tpu backends. optimal performance: by using tf.shape, the dimensions remain symbolic within the same compiled executable. this avoids the "re-compilation trap" and allows xla to optimize the hardware kernels once for all input sizes. while the nightly fix prevents the failure, using tf.shape ensures the system remains lean and statically sound.

hi , apologies for the delay, and thanks for raising your concern here we appreciate your detailed observation. you are right that cuda kernels execute asynchronously, and premature tensor deallocation could theoretically lead to race conditions or use-after-free issues. however, tensorflow manages this safely without [inline code] calls in functions such as [inline code] , [inline code] , or [inline code] . instead, tensorflow uses an event-based deferred deallocation mechanism implemented in the gpu runtime. this design ensures that memory used by a cuda kernel is not freed until the kernel’s execution has completed. here are a few references that explain this behavior: - [tensorflow gpu event manager source (gpu event mgr.cc)]( [url] handles deferred frees and event polling. - [tensorflow gpu guide]( [url] describes asynchronous gpu memory behavior and allocator configuration. - [nvidia tensorflow user guide (pdf)]( [url] documents the tf gpu allocator=cuda malloc async option for asynchronous allocation and freeing. so even though there are no explicit synchronization calls in those executor methods, tensorflow ensures correctness through deferred-free and event-driven synchronization mechanisms in the gpu runtime. i hope this clarifies your concern. thank you!

hi , this is intended behavior for xla. the xla compiler is optimized for numerical computations (linear algebra) and does not natively support [inline code] types or operations like [inline code] that rely on hash tables and variable-length data. when you use [inline code] , you force the entire function context to run on the xla device. since the function input is a string tensor, xla attempts to create an entry point ( [inline code] ) for a string, which fails because xla kernels only support numerical types (floats, ints, bools). solution: you must separate the preprocessing (strings - ints) from the computation (ints - output). the preprocessing should run in standard tensorflow (cpu), and the resulting integers can then be passed to the jit-compiled function. here is the corrected architectural pattern: python import tensorflow as tf from tensorflow import keras from tensorflow.keras import layers class testmodel(keras.model): def init (self): super(). init () 1. preprocessing (cpu/standard runtime) self.number encoding = layers.stringlookup(vocabulary=["1", "2", "3", "4", "5"]) 2. computation (xla compatible) self.embedding = layers.embedding(input dim=self.number encoding.vocabulary size(), output dim=64) def call(self, inputs): step a: convert strings to ints (not compiled) encoded = self.number encoding(inputs) step b: run math on xla (compiled) return self.compiled embedding(encoded) .function(jit compile=true) def compiled embedding(self, encoded inputs): this function only sees integers, so xla is good return self.embedding(encoded inputs) inputs = tf.constant(["3", "1", "4", "2", "5"]) model = testmodel() output = model(inputs) print("success:", output.shape)

hi, yes, this is default and expected behavior in tensorflow. those early gpu/cuda “warnings” appear before the logger (absl::initializelog()) is initialized, so tf cpp min log level doesn’t suppress them. they are harmless messages, and once tensorflow is fully initialized, the log level works normally. unfortunately, there’s no built-in way to completely hide these startup messages without redirecting stderr or modifying the tf source. they can be annoying during debugging, but they do not indicate a real error and won’t appear again during model training or normal execution.

from the linker documentation the –b symbolic(functions) options are intended for specialized dynamic objects and are not recommended for general use. although these options can reduce runtime relocation overhead, they can compromise bindings that expect interposition. to reduce runtime relocation processing, the creation of a version definition or use of direct bindings is recommended over using these options. in other words it is an optimization technique to shorten relocation times. framework.so is by design supposed to be imported by many other libraries (all tf's own python bindings and many other libs which depend on it). yes, it does not work well, but that is a different discussion, and a decision which had been made many years ago, so we are stuck with it. main point of this issue is the need to control and limit exported symbols by providing version script to framework.so.2, though matching pr ( [url] introduces version script which still exports [everything ]( [url] yes, the general idea of properly controlling exported symbols is what we would like to have, the bigger [inline code] has it (but still exports tons of stuff), [inline code] by design is a small subset of tf, that can be exported by anyone, thus its entire body is exported. in other words, the version script part did not change the behavior, the symbolic-functions arg broke mac build, and i don't think the symbolic optimization technique is appropriate for a binary which is being loaded all over the place (both automatically via dynamic section resolution and often manually by [inline code] in downstream projects), as it is likely to brake downstream projects in weird ways.

hi, i'd like to work on this issue. my proposed approach is to raise an explicit error when torch.func.{grad, vjp, jvp} are called under torch.inference mode(), rather than silently returning incorrect zero results. rationale: - inference mode() is explicitly designed for non-differentiable inference workloads (with autograd disabled), so it's inconsistent to allow gradient-related transforms like grad/vjp/jvp to run in this mode. - an explicit error is far safer than silent failure (which can lead to hard-to-debug issues for users), aligning with pytorch's philosophy of "fail fast" for invalid usage. i'm happy to adjust the approach (e.g., temporarily disable inference mode to return correct results instead) if the maintainers prefer that direction.

hi and , i'm hoping that we can get this to work rather than error. i've proposed changes [here]( [url] and a use case: molecular dynamics (but any sensitivity analysis or ode use case could benefit). thanks!

hi and , i totally understand that it would be convenient for use cases like molecular dynamics, sensitivity analysis, and ode solvers if these function transforms could work under torch.inference mode(). however, i believe we should raise an explicit error instead of silently returning incorrect zeros or trying to make them work. torch.inference mode() is explicitly designed for inference-only workloads with autograd fully disabled. running gradient-related transforms like grad, vjp, and jvp inside this mode is semantically inconsistent. allowing them to run silently not only produces wrong results but also violates the design contract of inference mode. for users who need differentiation while avoiding unnecessary gradient tracking, torch.no grad() is already the appropriate choice. therefore, i think the correct fix is to detect when these apis are used under inference mode() and raise a clear error message to prevent silent failures.

inductor fallback random=0 gives the same result, so this is likely not related to rng handling. the root cause is [inline code] defaults to true and tf32 inherently has fewer mantissa bits than fp32 causing precision error to accumulate across multiple conv layers. which is large enough to flip classification labels and compromise model reliability i'd recommend applying torch.compile to both the training and inference of the model, to ensure numerical behavior stays the same and reduce the chance of model reliability issue.

bitwise equivalence is not yet expected here. closing.

hi -logic i understand that bitwise equivalence is not a current goal, but there is a significant usability issue here. the core problem is that inductor shape padding is marketed as a memory alignment optimization, yet it implicitly toggles precision-altering hardware paths (tf32) without the user's explicit consent. if a user has already set torch.set float32 matmul precision('highest'), they expect fp32 precision. if shape padding=1 overrides this global precision setting to enable tensor cores, it violates the principle of least astonishment. at the very least, could this side-effect be documented, or should inductor respect the global matmul precision setting when deciding whether to pad for tensor cores? closing this as "intended" ignores the fact that users are losing 13 bits of precision silently.

this feels like a [url] topic. imo what is missing in your proposal, is why such thing is needed. this is very technically challenging problem(and significantly impact teams ability to innovate), and i can't imagine a real world scenario where something like that would be necessary/beneficial. if one plans to spawn a distributed training, they should be able to install the same version of software on all node, shouldn't they?

thanks for the thoughtful feedback, — that makes sense. you're absolutely right that homogeneous deployments are the expected and recommended model. the intent here is not to support long-term mixed-version clusters. the motivation is more about failure diagnostics than compatibility. in practice, accidental version skew can happen during: rolling upgrades in long-running clusters ci or staging environments where cached environments differ across nodes shared research clusters where jobs inherit different module environments in those cases, the resulting failure mode is often: silent hang in collective ops low-level nccl/gloo errors mysterious mismatches that are difficult to trace back to version differences the proposal is not to enable version interoperability, but simply to detect incompatible protocol revisions early during init process group() and produce a clear error message. if the position is that mixed-version deployments are unsupported and we are comfortable with current failure modes, that's completely reasonable. i mainly wanted to clarify whether: this scenario is already guarded implicitly somewhere, or there would be interest in improving the diagnostic experience for accidental skew. if this discussion would be better suited for dev-discuss, i'm happy to move it there.

i think the issue is that there would be a high likelihood that if someone ever made a change that affected the 'protocol' (which is a nebulous concept since there are multiple comms layers with their own protocols), they would probably not know to update the 'version' tag. i don't think it would be all that useful to just have one version tag per pytorch release either. also, we'd need to include things like nccl version or gloo version, which are underlying libraries used by pytorch. so i think this is probably not too actionable.

thanks and for the thoughtful feedback — this is very helpful. the maintenance burden and protocol boundary concerns make sense. if version tracking isn't consistently updated or clearly scoped across communication layers, it could create misleading guarantees rather than improve reliability. given that, and the expectation of homogeneous deployments, i agree this is probably not actionable in its current form. appreciate you both taking the time to discuss this.

dynamo rewrites your dist.all reduce into [dist. functional collectives.all reduce]( [url] so the output will be changed under the hood to a asynccollectivetensor. we don't support branching on the type of the old dist.all reduce handle. you need to branch your eager logic and compile logic if you want to do that, using is compiling(). a simpler option is to use the functional collective api in eager as well.

it's also good to note that when you compile with async op=false, it will desugar to out t = ... out t waited = wait tensor(out t) where wait tensor is dropped as far as possible (i.e., before the first read) --- responding to... is there a way to do all reduce asynchronously it kind of already has the same properties of an async op=true call in eager. this is confusing, and i'm hoping to make this distinction more explicit in the torchcomms integration which does seem to solve the problems you mentioned above with the confusing return type.

[inline code] 3. calculate one element of a matmul $h {0...i} = h {0...i-1} h i$, where $h {0...i-1}$ is the result from the previous iteration. 4. [inline code] 5. write the calculated element of $h {0...i}$ to the result buffer. the barrier at step 2 is necessary to make sure all elements of $h i$ are written to memory before the matmul is calculated by any of the threads. barrier at step 4 is to make sure all threads wait until the whole matmul is calculated before any threads go ahead to calculate the next $h i$ and overwrite the values in the existing buffer for it before all threads got a chance to read the previous value out. if $a$ (and thus $h i$) has more elements in it than the number of threads in a threadgroup, then to me it seems like the barrier won't properly sync all the threads the way the algorithm requires. or is there something else i'm missing now? if there really is a race, then i think the solution is probably to make only one thread group operate on $a$, and each thread can calculate multiple elements if the number of elements is greater than the maximum thread group size. but of course if $a$ is batched, we can create a separate thread group for each batch. let me know what you think

i think your concern is valid, and the ambiguity here is exactly where correctness bugs tend to hide. the key question seems to be the synchronization scope, not metal vs cuda per se. [inline code] only provides ordering within a single threadgroup. if [inline code] (and thus [inline code] ) spans more elements than can be covered by one threadgroup, then multiple threadgroups will necessarily participate in producing or consuming [inline code] . in that case, the barriers at steps (2) and (4) cannot guarantee the global ordering that the algorithm assumes. in other words, the algorithm requires: 1. all elements of [inline code] to be fully materialized before any thread computes [inline code] 2. all elements of [inline code] to be fully consumed before any thread overwrites the buffer for [inline code] these are global synchronization requirements. a threadgroup-level barrier is insufficient unless we can prove that a single threadgroup owns the entire matrix (or batch slice). i agree this is not unique to metal — cuda has the same limitation — but cuda implementations typically avoid this pattern by either: 1. enforcing one block per matrix (with each thread handling multiple elements), or 2. introducing kernel boundaries between iterations to obtain a global sync. so if the current mps implementation allows multiple threadgroups to participate in a single [inline code] , then this is very likely a real race, not just a theoretical one. the fact that it may “usually work” would be consistent with a latent synchronization bug. your proposed fix (one threadgroup per matrix, with striding inside the group) seems like the safest correctness-preserving approach, especially for non-batched or small-batch cases. kernel-splitting would also be a correctness-first alternative if performance allows. happy to be corrected if there's a guarantee that [inline code] never crosses a threadgroup boundary — but if not, i think this deserves a fix or at least a documented constraint.

what’s broken is that torch.compile (torchdynamo + inductor) miscompiles dtype-changing view() operations by treating them as safe aliasing views, even though they reinterpret storage at the byte level. which is why compiling an unrelated bf16 path or adding a “no-op” reshape changes the q6 k result (the compiled kernel ends up reading misaligned or stale bytes, yielding 0.0625 instead of 1.0) the proper fix in pytorch is to treat dtype-changing views as unsafe, either force a materializing copy (or alignment/lifetime barrier) when view(dtype) changes element size, or tighten alias analysis so inductor cannot reuse storage across such reinterpret casts. a practical workaround is to avoid view(dtype) under torch.compile: replace it with to(dtype) (copy), or insert clone() / contiguous() right after any dtype reinterpretation, or keep gguf dequant code out of compiled regions; all of these prevent buffer reuse and restore correctness at the cost of a small copy.

does this diagnosis and proposed fix sound correct to you? should [inline code] that changes element size be treated as unsafe in inductor (copy-on-view or a no-alias/lifetime barrier), rather than a normal aliasing view, to prevent cross-graph buffer reuse and miscompilation in cases like gguf dequant?

torchdynamo generates identical cache keys (codeid) for factory-wrapped functions (like wrap compile) because it hashes the code object without hashing the bound closure variables, causing the second compilation (dequantize blocks q6 k) to falsely hit the cache of the first (dequantize blocks bf16) and execute with stale static assumptions (e.g. wrong shape/stride constants for view(dtype)). -in code review & audit: pr first off, excellent root-cause analysis. you successfully traced a very stealthy cache-poisoning bug back to its origin: dynamo treating dynamically generated functions (closures) as identical because it was only hashing the code object. the added test case (test factory created functions separate pgo state) is bulletproof and perfectly isolates the issue. however, while the fix works for this specific reproducer, there are a couple of system-level edge cases regarding python's cell semantics and distributed caching that we need to harden before this gets merged into main. here is my audit: 1. the valueerror blast radius (cell empty exception) in python, accessing c.cell contents on an uninitialized cell doesn't return none; it raises valueerror: cell is empty. in your implementation: [code block] because the try...except wraps the entire tuple comprehension, if even one cell in the closure is uninitialized, the exception is thrown, the comprehension aborts, and closure hash silently falls back to none. this leaves the function vulnerable to the exact same cache collision bug we are trying to fix if it happens to have mixed initialized/uninitialized cells. fix: handle the valueerror on a per-cell basis. 2. the ephemerality of id() and remote pgo in get closure content: [code block] using id(content) is a classic trap in caching layers. id() is tied to the memory address of the object, which is strictly tied to the lifecycle of the current python process. if a user is leveraging remote pgo (which relies on codeid to serialize state across distributed nodes or disk caches—see automatic dynamic remote pgo in pgo.py), id() will result in non-deterministic hashes across different runs. this will cause remote cache misses and trigger silent recompilations. fix: if the object isn't a code object, attempt to hash its actual value (if it is immutable/hashable) before falling back to id(). we want to preserve determinism as much as possible for remote caches. suggested code refactoring here is a hardened version of your logic that handles both the per-cell valueerror and improves the determinism of the hash: [code block] summary the pr is fundamentally correct in its approach. update the valueerror handling to prevent the whole hash generation from aborting. patch id() to prioritize actual object hashing for remote cache determinism. drop these updates in, and from an engineering perspective, this is a beautiful fix to a nasty bug. great job tracking this down!

for [inline code] , [inline code] and [inline code] models: imo, comparing regressions using absolute binary equal operations is too strict. it does not allow for small / non-meaningful numerical differences. a single pixel in the mask that changes from (using a threshold of 0.5 as an example) 0.4999999 to 0.500001 would trigger the whole test to fail. even if computationally those are equivalent and just small numerical discrepancies have been introduced. it is practice the standard to use similarity metrics in those cases. the same as we do with numerical comparisons for other models. so, imo all accuracy tests for masks should be using similarity metrics and not absolute equal comparisons. and looking at nightlies is a half-story. the problem is that this test have been failing consistently for a long while. and this can be observed into this signal flakiness, where about 10% of the time we got errors that [inline code] was failing.
